data.title: One Stop Systems: Pure Rugged-Edge AI Play, I Got My Eye On You
data.description:
Introduction

One Stop Systems (OSS) recently announced its full-year 2025 results, which at first glance look worrisome, but when you dig deeper, you can see the company is becoming a lot more streamlined with a massive potential to be unlocked in the current niche operations.

The Company

OSS designs and sells rugged edge-compute and storage hardware for applications in AI, ML, autonomy, and sensor-processing. There is a large focus on defense and other harsh environment applications where connection is vital. The company offers ruggedized servers, compute accelerators, flash-storage arrays, and other storage-acceleration software.

Its products are deployed close to the source, which includes aircraft, ships, vehicles, drones, and other autonomous systems.

Performance

Let’s take a look at the top-line performance throughout the year. We can see a sharp drop in the last quarter of the year, which may be concerning and worth a look into more deeply. To save you the trouble, the reason for such a drop off is that OSS disclosed that their other revenue segment, the Bressner Technologies, was sold on December 30th ’25, leading to a distorted performance. OSS decided to sell Bressner as it looks to streamline its business and focus on high-margin segments like Edge AI and other AI applications. Having acquired Bressner in 2018 for $5.6m, selling it for $22.4m is not a bad deal at all.

If we focus on the post-divestiture operations, we can see that revenue growth came in at around 31% y/y. For Q4 alone, the company saw revenue growth coming in at a whopping 70.2%.

Looking at the company’s profitability and how it progressed over the last year, we can see that the company was not profitable, but the operations have improved significantly.

If we take a look at the separated business without Bressner, which by the way, was a profitable business in 2025, we can see that the growth in revenue far exceeded the growth in costs and expenses, which led to a massive improvement in gross and operating margins. Cost of revenues declined 23% while operating expenses increased by around 19%. What does this tell us about gross margins? It supports the view that what remains as OSS has much better unit economics, and most likely a good reason for the Bressner sale. In Q4, the company hit a record gross margin of 58.5%.

Going over the company’s financial situation, we can see that OSS finished the year with around $31.1m in cash and equivalents and no debt, except for some operating lease liabilities of around $1.2m. This is a fantastic position to be in going forward as a leaner business that can focus all of its capital resources to further its growth. When the business is still not profitable, a light balance sheet with no debt obligations is ideal. Looking at the company’s cash flow statement, we can see that cash from operations is quite deep in the red. This is due to the company selling a lot more of the product on credit, as its accounts receivable increased by over $7m y/y. To me, this sounds like a transitory and not an indefinite change, but I guess time will tell. This sort of cash burn is not going to be sustainable.

Overall, we can see that the company is not going to be the same as it was last year. Obviously. OSS lost essentially half of its revenue after the divestiture, but what is left is a company that is a lot cleaner overall, and the focus on improving profitability is very much doable, seeing how much the company’s margins improved post-sale.

Comments on the Outlook

Guidance

The management provided guidance for full-year 2026, which looks quite decent, with some notable developments. First, the management expects to see decent growth in revenue. The range given was 20%-25%. Gross margins are to be around 40%, and the company expects to continue to show positive EBITDA and adjusted EBITDA figures. Revenue growth and margins guided are below those of the Q4 performance; however, the overall guidance tells us that the company is still growing, and its unit economics are well above the legacy consolidated margin profile.

Defense

This is the segment that the company sees the most potential in, and I couldn’t agree more. They have such a niche offering like ruggedized AI compute, which is the exact appeal for militaries. Militaries often need computing that is close to the action, which means in places where there is very limited cloud connectivity and high ping. The hardware must survive heat, vibration, dust, and shock. The commercial servers are not designed for such environments, like battlefields, or even vehicle-mounted conditions. Rugged systems are designed specifically to work through all the mentioned hazards.

With OSS separating, this is the niche it wants to continue to focus on going forward: high-performance AI compute and storage at the tactical edge, where the customer needs to have both the performance and survivability, reducing the dependence on fragile backhaul links and improving overall resilience.

The management said that the company is gaining traction across many military programs and autonomous systems. This is important because these multi-year programs are the best way for the company to become a recurring platform revenue. I will be looking out for the company’s backlog improvement going forward.

The company said, in the latest earnings, that it already has the P-8 Poseidon aircraft contract under its belt, totaling $65m in contracted revenue to be realized through 2027, and another new agreement for US Army combat vehicles to design and develop ruggedized integrated compute systems, as well as visualization systems.

Commercial

On top of what I think is the most promising end-market, commercial is seen to move some decent product too, specifically in aerospace, robotics, autonomous construction, and healthcare. The management said that they see a growing demand for high-performance computing at the edge for autonomy, sensor fusion, and real-time decision-making. In the same earnings report, the company said it won a robotics customer in February of 2026, where OSS displaced an incumbent solution, showing that the company continues to win commercial design-ins and is not solely relying on the defense demand.

Overall, I am quite curious to see what the company is going to look like, now that it is becoming a pure-play edge platform, with a very interesting niche. I always applaud when the management takes such a massive decision to divest a massive business segment to focus on what they think is the key growth driver of the company.

Risks and Closing Comments

Usually, when I see a company going all in on AI, ML, and other related end-markets, I become a bit worried, especially now that AI has become so prominent in so many different businesses that people are worrying that there might be a bubble. Following the money is not a bad move, and the money is going towards AI and its applications. However, I am not too worried in this case because of how niche the OSS product is. It is going to have a lot of demand in the future from the defense and commercial sectors, regardless of where AI itself is heading. Rugged environments require proper connectivity constantly.

Since the company also offers some sort of storage products, it is important to note that it is going to be affected quite a bit by the component costs, especially DRAM and SSD. The management already said that the impact is noticeable, so we need to pay attention to what happens in the future. This is still going to be a negative until additional capacity unlocks in 2027 and beyond.

I am interested in what the future looks like for OSS now that it is a much more streamlined business, but I am not ready to put down some money for now, and I am willing to follow along for the ride. I’d like to see margins improving further and cash flow getting closer to positive, which will signal that the operations are indeed better off separated.

data.title: Cheniere's Long-Term Boost From Global LNG Disruptions Largely Priced Into Stock
data.description:
Investment thesis

The massive disruptions in LNG supplies from the ME, as well as from Russia, have short-term as well as long-term implications for Cheniere's (LNG) outlook. In the short term, the disruption stemming from the Strait of Hormuz has the potential to replicate the LNG price spike we saw in 2021-2022 if the conflict continues for a prolonged period. In the longer term, Qatar is faced with a multi-year partial outage. The Chinese-Russian Power of Siberia 2 project is more likely to be implemented, and perhaps the timeline will be expedited based on recent reports. This leaves the Europeans with no choice but to remain heavily dependent on a potentially tight LNG market, with no choice but to buy the needed volumes, regardless of the prevailing price.

Among the potentially negative long-term factors I am looking at for Cheniere, I see early signs of the US shale patch stagnating, which could lead to rising natural gas prices in the US, which can potentially cut into Cheniere's profitability. With these considerations in mind, I see Cheniere's current share price as fully reflecting the potential fundamental upside, which is why I recently sold my position, and I am looking to buy again at some point, in a post-war new normal.

Going from a buy to a hold

I last covered Cheniere in December, when I had it as a buy on a significant pullback in its share price, which I found to have not been supported by its company-specific profile, as well as the external market outlook. At the time, I was watching the tightening of the natural gas market in Europe as a sign of higher potential LNG prices. Cheniere's stock price is up nearly 60% since I wrote that article, so I decided to take profits on my position, and I am currently designating the stock as a hold.

I should mention that while most of the gain happened in March, Cheniere also saw its share price increase by 25% between the time I wrote the December article and the end of February, when the war started. It is important to highlight this because I believe the increase in its share price before the war started is a reflection of the market's perceptions of Cheniere's fundamentals in normal times.

Cheniere's profitability and growth potential profile

For the full year of 2025, Cheniere saw a revenue increase of 27%, with earnings growing by 64% relative to 2024.

As we can see, the improvement in its financial results did not come primarily as a result of LNG sales growth, since its deliveries only increased by 4%. Factors such as better pricing and lower ramp-up costs played a more significant role.

Cheniere is on track to add more LNG export capacity this year, at its CCL Stage 3 project, which should further boost its revenue and profit growth, in addition to the sizable increase in LNG prices we are experiencing this year, based on Europe's TTF spot price, which tends to reflect LNG spot prices.

Beyond this year, it has significant expansion projects in the pipeline, which make this a growth stock, an increasingly rare occurrence among major oil & gas companies.

Other financial highlights of note for 2025 include Cheniere's debt and debt-servicing profile, relative to its other financial metrics, which is an important trend to watch, especially for a company involved in continued expansion. Cheniere's net interest expenses came in at $946 million, which comes out to 4.7% of revenues for the year. It is a significant improvement on 2024, when interest costs came in at 6.4% of revenues. It is just below the threshold of 5%, where I consider a company to be in danger of seeing a financial vicious cycle develop, where an excessive portion of a company's financial resources needs to be allocated to servicing debt. The company's long-term debt held steady in 2025 compared with the prior year, at $22.51 billion.

Short-term & long-term LNG market considerations

With every week that passes where Qatari LNG exports are curtailed, the world is losing about 2 Bcm of natural gas supplies. In other words, we've lost about 8 Bcm so far, and according to various estimates, even if the war were to end today, it might take up to eight more weeks before Qatar returns to full production capacity. The new capacity will reflect the roughly one-fifth that was lost due to an Iranian attack, which will take up to five years to repair. In other words, even if the war ends today, there is a loss in LNG supplies of roughly 35 Bcm from Qatar alone this year, when we include the fact that even when it resumes production, it will be producing at 80% of its prior capacity. The way I see it, with every week that passes without a resolution to this war, the net effect on the LNG market this year is that the average price will rise, with all else held equal.

In addition to the loss of Qatari LNG, there are other LNG producers in the region that had to halt production, such as the UAE. There are also the constant attacks on Russian natural gas exports due to the war in Ukraine, which might be reducing natural gas availability this year and perhaps beyond. With every day that the wars in the ME & in Ukraine continue, there is a chance of further long-term damage to natural gas export infrastructure that could take years to rebuild.

Aside from disruptions to existing natural gas export facilities, there are valid questions, in my view, about whether prior plans, like Qatar's path to doubling its LNG output by 2030, will still be in place. It is possible, in my view, that those plans will now face massive delays. The focus will probably be on investing in restoring lost capacity from the war. Qatar might want to do both at the same time, but the capital involved might be hard to come by, especially since Qatar is currently experiencing a lack of revenue inflows as its main export, namely natural gas and derived products, has come to a standstill.

On the demand side, I am increasingly of the view that China and other Asian nations will gradually capture most of Russia's natural gas exports, which will lead to a cut in demand growth for all non-Russian sources of natural gas in the region. The Power of Siberia 2 pipeline, which might divert up to 50 Bcm/year of natural gas from Western Russia to China, seems to be picking up momentum now that the risk of disruption from other sources of natural gas has become obvious. China is reportedly allocating funds to build its part of the pipeline in the next five-year investment plan. Once there is a clear green light on both ends to start the construction of the pipeline, with a price mechanism deal in place, the energy and perhaps economic divorce between Russia and the EU will probably be complete.

While Asia might see reduced reliance on natural gas imports from the ME or from the Americas thanks to Russia's pivot East, Europe will likely become more dependent on LNG than ever. It will become a buyer with few alternatives but to offer any price needed to secure sufficient volumes. Furthermore, I expect Europe's demand for natural gas to start rebounding, after four years of steep decline, as its governments will aim to relaunch their struggling economies.

The rebound in demand is unlikely to happen this year, given that the entire world is increasingly likely to see a decline in economic growth due to the negative effects of higher energy prices. Assuming that by next year we will be on a solid path of economic recovery, with conflict-related disruptions and other negative factors mostly resolved, I believe that Europe will push for reindustrialization, which will require higher volumes of natural gas imports in the longer term.

A particularly relevant factor in terms of long-term global natural gas market trends that may affect Cheniere is the possibility that US natural gas output may stagnate and start declining. Recent Baker Hughes drilling data shows that drilling activities in the US are stagnating at a low level of activity.

The latest monthly EIA natural gas output report suggests that output is stagnating, with no significant output growth since March of last year. At the same time, America's LNG export capacity keeps expanding, with Cheniere playing a major role in the trend. If US natural gas output stagnates and it starts declining in the next few years, we may end up with an excess LNG export capacity situation, not to mention much higher domestic natural gas prices.

Investment implications

The differentiating factor between my core oil & gas positions, namely Suncor (SU), CNQ (CNQ) & Equinor (EQNR) on one hand, and my non-core positions that Cheniere belonged to, mostly comes down to two factors. The core companies in which I built my oil & gas position I bought at a low-cost basis relative to their current price. I am currently up well over 100% on average on these stocks. The reason I picked these companies as my core position is in large part due to the generous and secure dividends that they offer.

As we can see, Cheniere is mostly performing in line with its peers so far this year, except for Equinor, which is outperforming the group at the moment.

My decision to start selling peripheral oil & gas stocks into the current rally, before I start reducing my core positions, is not as much driven by considerations related to which stocks I believe might perform better through this crisis, but rather which positions I want to be left with once the crisis ends. The obvious answer is that the stocks that also come with a healthy dividend are the preferred option. My current Suncor shares yield about 7.5%, relative to my average cost basis, while Cheniere shares currently have a dividend yield of under 1%.

I should also note that Equinor, which is Cheniere's main competition in the European natural gas market, is currently trading at a forward P/E ratio of 14, versus over 20 for Cheniere, despite the much more generous dividend yield, which is 3.5% for Equinor. The one advantage that Cheniere has that goes into its valuation is its growth profile. That growth assumption is based on expectations of continued US natural gas output growth, which might or might not materialize, as I already pointed out.

Having sold my position in this stock, I am now looking for a new entry point, which I see as the pre-war all-time high that it reached over a year ago of $250/share. I intend to pick up shares incrementally from there if it continues to decline. This is unlikely to happen until after the Iran war ends. While I am looking to pick up Cheniere shares in the near term, it is unlikely to become one of my core positions in the oil & gas segment for the reasons I cited.

Risks to my thesis

While I am operating under the assumption that by the start of summer the Iran war situation will be settled somehow and ME energy exports will begin to rebound, it is possible that this will not happen, in which case Cheniere's share price might still have a long way to go on the upside this year. My decision to sell will be proven to have been premature. My decision to revisit buying this stock only after the war and energy flows are restored will also prove to be a missed opportunity in that case.

Conclusions

Regardless of what happens this year, one takeaway that we need to factor into Cheniere's longer-term outlook is that, at least in the case of Qatar, there is a multi-year LNG capacity outage, which will keep markets tighter than expected for years to come. Cheniere is set to benefit from potentially higher LNG prices in the long term. The combination of its robust LNG production growth profile and higher LNG prices, at least for the next few years, makes this stock worth considering for investment once it declines to pre-war levels.

data.title: PVH Corp.: Earnings Power Is Materially Undervalued (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
PVH Corp. (PVH) reported the company’s fiscal Q4 results from the November-January period on the 31st of March. The Tommy Hilfiger and Calvin Klein owner’s growth remains slow, but earnings remain resilient despite a challenging operating environment. The earnings outlook is stabilizing despite a volatile tariff environment and uncertain brand value, in my opinion making the stock’s risk-to-reward very good as PVH trades at an incredibly cheap valuation. Aggressive share repurchases continue to push up PVH’s EPS.

I initiated the stock at a Hold rating in my previous March 2025 article on the stock, titled “PVH Corp. Q4 Preview: I Recommend High Caution.” The stock has since returned 1%; meanwhile, the S&P 500 (SP500) has returned 13%.

PVH Q4 Review

PVH’s growth has remained fairly anemic. The Q4 report showed 6% reported revenue growth to $2.51 billion due to a weaker USD but reflected flat growth in constant currency underneath. Tommy Hilfiger’s revenues grew by 1% in constant currency, while Calvin Klein’s even declined by -1%. Growth was very slightly slower compared to previous quarters, as PVH reported revenues that were up slightly in constant currency for the full fiscal year. Overall, the sales performance continues to reflect quite a weak brand performance in PVH’s two main names.

The result still beat Wall Street’s consensus by $71 million, as PVH previously guided sales to decline slightly in constant currency in Q4. Not very much was expected.

Margins declined slightly but remained relatively stable regardless of slow sales and a challenging tariff environment. Considering the environment, I believe that PVH's margin performance was good. The gross margin declined by 60 basis points to 57.6%, reflecting tariff pressure, increased promotions, and other factors. The adjusted operating margin declined by 30 basis points to 10.0%, as SG&A efficiencies offset a part of the gross margin decline.

In total, adjusted EBIT grew by $6 million year-on-year to $250 million, leading the quarter’s $3.82 adjusted EPS to beat Wall Street’s consensus by $0.52. I believe that PVH’s Q4 financials were good when compared to low market expectations going into the report.

I believe that the Q4 report signaled better stabilization in PVH’s future earnings. After earnings have declined noticeably in the past couple of years, PVH now guides the adjusted EBIT margin to stay consistent at 8.8% in FY2026. Notably, a stable margin performance implies good underlying margin gains, as PVH faces an expected 215 basis point headwind from tariffs for the fiscal year. Earnings are stabilizing despite an uncertain operating environment, where tariffs weigh on margins and consumer confidence weighs on the sales outlook.

PVH’s FY2026 guidance assumes a 15% global tariff from February 24th forward, reflecting Trump’s Section 122 tariff. The Section 122 tariff can only remain in place for 150 days without Congress’s approval for an extension, though. The Supreme Court’s previous ruling to block Trump’s IEEPA tariffs could help boost margins, especially from FY2027 forward, which the guidance doesn't yet account for.

Sales are guided to be flat or increase slightly in constant currency in FY2026, still reflecting a slow performance – on top of volatility in consumer confidence, an underlying concern regarding Tommy Hilfiger’s and Calvin Klein’s brand relevancy remains. The first quarter is expected to be even weaker, with a guided low single-digit sales decline. Over the long term, brand revitalization is still important for PVH to maintain margins as well; without constant cost savings, growth alongside cost inflation is needed to maintain margins. The Q4 report didn’t yet give clear visibility into improved brand power.

Positively, Tommy Hilfiger’s brand searches have started to stabilize according to Google Trends, and Calvin Klein has shown a sharp spike in searches during February and March. Whether improved Internet search volume will translate into improved sales remains to be seen, though. PVH’s guidance suggests that such a translation won’t happen.

For now, while the earnings outlook is stable for FY2026, the focus remains on PVH’s ability to boost Tommy Hilfiger’s and Calvin Klein’s brand visibility to stabilize earnings over the long term as well.

PVH Stock Is Materially Undervalued; Share Repurchases Create Further Upside

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for PVH.

I again estimate sales growth to stabilize at a slow 1% level. I now estimate profitability using adjusted EBIT and expect the margin to decline slightly to 8% from FY2030 forward. While tariff pressure should start to subside, the estimate accounts for a moderate further decline in brand value.

The cash flow conversion should remain fairly good, as PVH’s CapEx is moderate.

The estimates put PVH’s fair value at $115.9, suggesting 66% upside as of writing. The stock is, in my opinion, highly undervalued; even with the assumption that earnings will decline moderately in the next few years, the stock has very clear upside based on cash flow. PVH stock only trades at a 5.8 adjusted P/E at the midpoint of the FY2026 guidance. Such an earnings multiple reflects very low expectations of PVH’s forward performance.

On top, I believe that the undervaluation makes PVH’s share repurchase program very attractive, creating further upside potential. The company repurchased $560 million worth of shares during 2025 and plans to purchase at least $300 million more in 2026. Repurchases are quick to reduce PVH’s outstanding shares; weighted average shares declined from 54.8 million to 45.8 million during FY2025, boosting the EPS by approximately 20%.

The fair value estimate is up from $92.5 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 8.40%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 6.27% interest rate, 200 basis points above the 10-year bond yield (US10Y). I assume quite a high 50% long-term debt-to-equity ratio as PVH remains highly leveraged.

For the cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.27% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the U.S. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.36. With a liquidity premium of 0.3%, the cost of equity stands at 10.25% and the WACC at 8.40%.

Takeaway

After PVH’s Q4 report, the company looks like an attractive investment. While sales growth remains slow due to Tommy Hilfiger’s and Calvin Klein’s turbulent brand relevancy, earnings are starting to stabilize despite a challenging operating environment. Stable earnings, a very cheap earnings multiple, and aggressive share buybacks create significant upside potential. I upgrade my rating on PVH stock to Buy but suggest watching Tommy Hilfiger’s and Calvin Klein’s brand performance closely going forward.

data.title: Netflix: Going All In Ahead Of Earnings (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Although Netflix (NFLX) stock is up about 25% from the February lows, shares are still nearly 30% below all-time highs.

This once perfectly clean story has become much noisier in the past six months, in light of a failed Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) takeover attempt and amid increasing concerns about engagement.

After all of that, I find that, in reality, nothing has changed. Which means Netflix is a 20%+ EPS growth story for years to come, it's trading at a significant discount, and there are several positive catalysts in the near term.

Bottom line, I'm upgrading Netflix to a "Strong Buy."

The Bear Thesis

Netflix's stock is still in a ~30% drawdown from its June 2025 highs. While many stocks in the market are in deep drawdowns, a broad market selloff is a tiny part of the story.

There are two key issues that the bears are leaning on, the primary one being engagement. They argue that Netflix's attempt at acquiring Warner Bros. is a sign of weakness that shows management is desperate to find new growth drivers. They then lean on data, which we'll get to in a second, to support their claim that engagement isn't growing, as social media and competitors take a larger piece of the time & attention pie.

The second, smaller concern, is saturation. At 325 million global subscribers, Netflix is pushing the ceiling as one of the world's largest paid subscription services based on subscriber count. Though impressive, they would say the future growth runway is limited.

Engagement Concerns Lack Important Nuance

The truth is, if you take a shallow look at engagement metrics, it's difficult to counter the bearish claims.

From January 2023 to January 2026, Netflix "only" gained 1.3% share of viewing based on Nielsen data, while streaming gained over 9 points.

Meanwhile, Netflix's What We Watched report shows that total hours watched on Netflix are up by less than 7% from H2-23 to H2-25.

So, what does this mean for Netflix investors?

Management said countless times that engagement is one of their north stars, as it reflects the significance of Netflix to users in their daily lives. However, I argue that not all engagement is created equal.

Which is more valuable to a user, a total of one hour of engagement broken into pieces throughout the day scrolling Instagram Reels (META), or watching a one-hour episode with the family in the evening? From my perspective, very few people would be willing to pay a monthly subscription for their Reels consumption, but very many would be willing to pay for their Netflix (325 million people, to be exact).

Indeed, on the recent earnings call, management called out the "quality" of engagement as a key metric:

Beyond view hours all hours of engagement are not the same, and we really care about the quality of that engagement. For example, we said in the letter, live programming is an example where any given hour of entertainment has the potential to deliver outsized value. That's certainly also the case when you have a huge fan of a particular series or a movie. And we, as viewers, we feel this intuitively, right? We feel that excitement, that difference, that value when we watch something that we've been anticipating and we just can't wait to watch.

And as a business, we've become increasingly sophisticated, evolving our measures of that quality of engagement that we are delivering. It's very hard to do this, but we're getting better and better at it. And our primary quality metric, we achieved in '25 an all-time high for the service. We set a high goal for that metric and Bela and our content team stepped up and achieved that goal.

That means that we deliver more entertainment value for our members. And that shows up in core metrics like acquisition, like retention. Retention is among the best in the industry, and we just completed a quarter where churn improved year-on-year. Customer satisfaction is at all-time high. We also saw strong member growth. So we really manage more and more towards that complete understanding of value delivered because it's really what translates best and directly to revenue growth, and it better captures the overall health of our business.

Forgive me for the long excerpt, but it's key to understand that the two most critical metrics for a subscription service like Netflix are churn and acquisition. On both of those, Netflix is the best in the industry, by far.

In my mind, there's no chance Netflix would have taken the recent price hikes if it saw any deterioration in that "quality" of engagement.

Saturation & Growth Runway Concerns Miss Two Key Aspects

At 325 million subscribers, Netflix, to the best of my knowledge, is the largest entertainment subscription service in the world in terms of paid subscriber count, edging Spotify (SPOT), which has 290 million.

While this certainly reflects significant market penetration, it's far from meaning there's no more runway left for more subscriber growth. In fact, Netflix estimates they are in less than 50% of connected TV households. Further, in the WSJ leak, the internal target was 410 million subscribers by 2030, which would reflect about a 5% annual CAGR from 2025.

While Netflix is indeed in most U.S. households, there are many regions where Netflix isn't as penetrated. And, more than any of its competitors, Netflix has built local production capabilities that are helping it conquer different cultures. Some recent moves include the BTS reunion, focusing on the K-pop audience, and the World Baseball Classic in Japan.

In my view, there's significant runway for subscriber additions, but even so, it's only one part of Netflix's growth algorithm, which includes price increases and advertising.

The addition of advertising to the mix allows Netflix to push ARPU higher without losing customers, as it "catches" them if they get outpriced from a certain tier.

We can see that since the launch of advertising, Netflix has been much more comfortable raising prices, and it's working. ARPU surpassed $12.0 in 2025, the largest jump since 2021. Meanwhile, advertising revenues exceeded $1.5 billion in 2025 and are set to at least double in 2026.

And, despite the criticism, Netflix remains cheaper than its peers, especially relative to content spend and viewing hours.

This reflects the "triple benefits" of advertising: it's easier to gain and maintain customers with a lower-priced plan. It's less risky to raise prices on higher-priced plans. And, you can drive revenue growth without asking more from your subscribers.

Valuation

At $96 per share, Netflix trades below 30 times forward earnings (before the guidance upgrade), and below 25 times next year's estimates.

This puts Netflix at a ~21% discount to its five-year average, and with expectations for a multi-year 20%+ EPS growth trajectory, Netflix is trading around a 1.3x PEG ratio.

I estimate Netflix's price target at $115, based on a 30x multiple on 2027 estimates, and see this as a 15%+ annual returns story for a long time.

Positive Catalysts Heading Into Q1 Earnings

I always look for the combination of attractive long-term prospects and positive near-term catalysts for a "Strong Buy" rating. We discussed the long term, now let's briefly go through the near term.

When Netflix provided guidance for Q1 and FY26, the Warner Bros. acquisition was considered, not in revenues, but in expenses related to it.

They saw a $275 million impact to operating income due to acquisition-related expenses, and while they did mention pricing as a growth driver for the year, many analysts believe they planned to do price hikes later in the year, once regulatory scrutiny subsides. They also planned to stop doing buybacks, preparing to fund the acquisition.

Heading into Q1, a guidance upgrade is all but certain, especially on the margin front, and possibly on revenue.

My Top10 tracker isn't tracking that strongly, but I have a theory that this is the result of content dispersion, and that this tracker is decreasing in its predictive value.

So, the bottom line, a beat & raise is likely coming, and so is management commentary on their recent content initiatives, and life after WBD. To me, this is a combination of net positive catalysts.

Conclusion

As the dust settles on the Warner Bros. acquisition noise, the bears are turning to misguided engagement and saturation concerns for their arguments.

In my view, the "quality" of engagement is only improving as Netflix diversifies into more content verticals and accelerates investments. Meanwhile, with the addition of ads, Netflix's business model has never been stronger, and the margin expansion ceiling is significantly pushed higher.

All that, and we're getting Netflix at a 20% discount, ahead of Q1 earnings, which I expect to have several positive catalysts.

As such, I'm upgrading Netflix to a "Strong Buy."

data.title: Euro Comes Out Swinging: Can The 'Trump Reversal' Sustain EUR/USD's Upside Bias?
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

The euro has come out swinging in early Wednesday trade, capitalizing on a sudden shift in market sentiment that has left the US dollar nursing its wounds.

After a period of intense geopolitical positioning, EUR/USD (EUR:USD) has surged back toward the 1.1600 handle, driven by a potent mix of "war optimism" and a hawkish recalibration of the European Central Bank (ECB) path.

Early Trade: The "Trump Reversal" in Action

The primary catalyst for the overnight move was a classic display of the "Trump Always Changes His Mind" (TACO) strategy.

US President Donald Trump’s comments suggesting the Middle East conflict could reach a resolution within two to three weeks, coupled with his call for Gulf states to forcibly reopen the Strait of Hormuz, triggered a sharp de-escalation trade.

From a technical standpoint, we saw the pair dip as low as 1.1446 before staging a rapid recovery to highs of 1.1563 during the late New York session. As we move through the European morning, the pair remains buoyed, trading with a clear upside bias as investors dump safe haven dollars in favor of riskier assets.

What's Driving the Pair?

Geopolitical De-escalation: The market is betting on a fall in Brent crude and an improvement in global risk appetite. If the war timeline holds, the global economy may dodge the worst-case recessionary scenarios, which is inherently euro-positive given the eurozone's sensitivity to energy prices.

ECB Hawk vs. Fed Dove: While the Fed appears content to sit on its hands or even lean toward a dovish repricing (with markets eyeing a potential December cut), the ECB is singing a different tune. Hawkish rhetoric from the likes of Schnabel and even the usually dovish Panetta has kept rate hike bets alive. Markets are currently pricing in approximately 63-71 basis points of tightening by year-end, creating a yield differential that favors the euro.

Inflation Realities: March eurozone CPI jumped to 2.5%, and while core inflation softened slightly to 2.3%, the headline pressure from food and energy is keeping the "hawks" at the ECB firmly in the driver's seat for now.

The Road Ahead: Upside Bias or Capped Range?

Looking forward, the immediate focus shifts to the US economic calendar, specifically the ADP Employment Change (expected at a soft 40k) and the ISM Manufacturing PMI. A weak jobs print would further validate the idea that the US economy is losing steam under the weight of the energy shock, likely piling more pressure on the greenback.

While the "Trump reversal" has provided the euro with a much-needed lifeline, the sustainability of this move rests on two factors: the actual reopening of the Strait of Hormuz and whether the ECB follows through on its hawkish threats. For now, the path of least resistance for EUR/USD appears to be higher, but in this "sell everything" environment, volatility remains the only certainty.

Potential Move and Key Levels

The momentum has clearly shifted, but we must remain cautious. While the upside bias is intact, I think we are likely looking at a capped range between 1.1490 and 1.1620.

  • Resistance: The 1.1595-1.1620 zone remains the major hurdle for bulls. A break above 1.1620 would signal a more structural shift in the pair's trajectory.
  • Support: On the downside, the euro needs to hold above 1.1520 to keep this intraday momentum alive. Any break back below 1.1450 would suggest that the "war optimism" was premature.

data.title: Meta: Here's Why I Believe Market Is Underestimating Meta's AI Monetization Story
data.description:
Introduction

Meta (META) has been one of the most lackluster Magnificent 7 stocks (-18% YTD), underperforming the S&P 500 (-7% YTD) by a substantial margin as of March 31. This has been attributable to:

  • Regulatory overhang (Big Tobacco moment fears) after a tribunal loss in Los Angeles on social media addiction, prompting investors’ worries on Section 230 violations,
  • AI CapEx and margin dilution concerns, with Meta guiding $115-135 billion in CapEx (+60% - 88% YoY) and declining OPM (from 48% to 41%), spooked investors’ fear that FCF turning negative while returns on AI spend (AI monetization) lag.
  • Widening Reality Labs (RL) losses ($6 billion in 4Q25) with an uncertain trajectory (Avocado model delays, 20% workforce reduction, termination of VR lab businesses), causing investors to question RL growth trajectory
  • Macroeconomic factors (the war in Iran causing risk-off trades)

Despite negative market sentiment, I remain bullish on Meta’s outlook in 2026, as I view 2026 as an inflection year of Meta’s AI monetization, with AI driving Meta’s business efficiency and top-line growth.

I believe that:

  1. Meta does not need to lead in frontier AI models (ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude), and Meta only needs to prove to investors that AI is driving business efficiency and top-line growth (higher ad loads, higher ad impression prices, more personalized recommendations, and end-to-end ad stack).
  2. Meta remains a quality gem with so many business optionalities (WhatsApp business monetization, Threads ads monetization, potential Instagram e-commerce like TikTok Shop, subscription of Instagram, sponsored listings in Facebook Marketplace, and so on), alongside
  3. Reality Labs scaling, including Ray-Ban Meta glasses entering volume shipment in 2H26/2027, and integration of Manus AI, Meta AI (Avocado), and Moltbook to create AI agents for its family of apps ecosystem, with potential monetization through subscriptions.

In my view, Meta’s core competitive advantage lies in its 3.58 billion MAU across its family apps (WhatsApp, Facebook, Instagram, Threads), creating cross-app ad-targeting and conversion synergies while also driving premium subscriptions.

With the stock price plunging by 18% YTD, I believe the market has already adequately priced the pessimistic scenario. With a forward FY27 P/E of 16x, the stock trades -1 SD below the 5-year historical average of 21x. I believe Meta presents an asymmetric upside opportunity at $530.

4Q25 Financial Analysis – a Decent Quarter Justifying Value of AI Capex

Overall, I Rate Meta’s 4Q25 Financials a B+

While I’m impressed by Meta’s robust top-line growth (+23.8%) during the quarter, I am somewhat disappointed about Meta’s widening Reality Labs losses and declining OPM (41.3% in 4Q25 vs. 48.3% in 4Q24).

Total revenue increased by 24% YoY to US$59.9bn, while net income grew by 9% YoY to US$22.8bn, outperforming market consensus estimates, anchored by AI driving material ad revenue growth (+24.2% YoY), offset by higher R&D expenses (29% vs 25% in 4Q24) and G&A expense ratio (6% vs 2% in 4Q24) from restructuring and higher infrastructure costs.

Looking ahead, Meta guides 1Q26 total revenue to grow by 26-34% YoY in 1Q26 but expects FY26 total revenue growth (23-25% growth) to decelerate below 1Q26 levels due to currency headwinds and high-base effects.

Nevertheless, I expect margins might come under pressure (dipping below 40%) given higher infrastructure depreciation costs and higher personnel costs related to compensation for AI talent.

Overall, I believe Meta remains in the midst of a multi-year investment cycle on AI (until 2028/29) to improve its internal business efficiency (Ads business); hence, I believe a capex (purchase of NVDA Vera Rubin GPUs or self-developed MTIA 3 Chips) to be US$115-135bn (+59-87% YoY) remains well warranted, considering demand for compute vastly exceeds supply.

With Meta’s track record of delivering ROIC above 9-10%, which is similar to its WACC of around 9-10%, I believe management isn’t destroying shareholders’ value.

Thesis 1: Meta Does Not Need to Lead in AI Frontier Models – It Only Needs to Prove AI Drives Business Efficiency and Top-Line Growth

I believe Meta does not need to compete against Anthropic Claude, Google Gemini, and OpenAI’s ChatGPT to develop Tier-1 frontier models.

Instead, I see Meta as an “AI application” beneficiary that effectively utilizes AI for internal workloads (i.e., advanced ranking, user intent predictions, ad recommendation, visual creative generation, ad performance, and campaign attribution), driving higher ad impressions volume and ad prices (ARPU) that ultimately contribute to accelerating top-line growth. This will be sufficient for the market to re-rate Meta, in my view.

In fact, I believe AI is already delivering measurable monetization performance for Meta from the following 3 dimensions.

  1. Ad Monetization: In 4Q25, improvements to the GEM ad ranking model (Meta Lattice sequence-learning architecture) drove a 3.5% increase in ad clicks on Facebook and an over 1% gain in conversions on Instagram. The incremental attribution model also drove a 24% increase in conversions.
  2. Engagement: Total watch time of Instagram Reels and Facebook videos grew by over 30% YoY and 7% lift in views of organic feed and video posts, thanks to the improvements of the feed and ranking system.
  3. Ad products: Advantage+ end-to-end AI suite continues to optimize performance and improve targeting efficiency (ROAS) for advertisers (see my AppLovin article). AI-powered video-generation tools hit a $10 billion revenue run rate (around 5% of Meta’s 2025 revenue).

That said, with Madison and Wall expecting AI-powered advertising to scale 63% YoY to $57 billion by 2026, outperforming 5% YoY of traditional advertising, I believe all these datapoint suggests Meta could easily see its advertising revenue growth re-accelerating 20% from 2026-28 considering its unique competitive advantage of 3.5 billion global MAU, Advantage+ (end-to-end automated ad stack) driving advertisers ROAS and auction density, alongside GEM iterations enhancing user engagement and conversion.

Hence, I see AI as a margin-expansion and pricing-power enabler for Meta inside the core ad business, which justifies its initial CapEx outlay.

Thesis 2: A Business With Abundant Real Optionality for Monetization Driving ARPU Expansions Beyond Ads

While Meta’s global and US ARPU ($16.56 and $66.81) remain substantially higher than its peers like Reddit (RDDT), Snap (SNAP), and Pinterest (PINS), I believe Meta’s family-of-apps moat (3.5B+ daily actives) gives it multiple high-ROIC levers that remain under-monetized.

Personally, I see a clear trajectory for Meta’s global/US ARPU to exceed $20/80 by the end of 2026 (4Q26). This includes:

  1. Instagram and Facebook premium subscription services, including Instagram Plus ($2/month) in Mexico, Japan, and the Philippines, that allow viewing stories in incognito mode. Assuming a 2% conversion rate for Instagram’s 3 billion MAU would yield (60m subscribers), this gives Meta an annual run rate of $2-3 billion in subscription revenue (around 1-1.5% of revenue in 2025)
  2. WhatsApp monetization through subscription and ads, with paid messaging crossing a $2 billion annual run rate in Q4 2025 (around 1% of revenue in 2025). Additionally, Business AIs in Mexico/Philippines (>1 million weekly conversations) are expanding, and Meta is also testing premium subscriptions for productivity/AI features
  3. Threads monetization: With over 400 million MAU on Threads, Meta has already enabled ad placements on Threads through Advantage+ and Ad Manager since Jan 2026, with updated third-party marketing APIs
  4. Sponsored listings on Facebook Marketplace, Instagram external checkout, e-commerce, etc.

Just to echo my thesis, Meta’s other revenue (WhatsApp paid messaging and Meta Verified subscription revenue) already hit $801M in 4Q25 (+54% YoY), growing at a pace quicker than ad revenue (+24% YoY), though it remains insignificant (1.4% of 4Q25 FOA revenue), but a high-single-digit to 10% revenue contribution assumption by 2030 remains on the cards, considering Meta’s aggressive pivot to subscription.

Overall, I believe the marginal cost for Meta to create incremental ad inventory or premium subscription services remains minimal (e.g., Existing Advantage+ and GEM model deployments).

I view such a move as diversifying Meta away from an ad-oriented franchise into a freemium services business (incorporating subscription), which makes strong economic sense considering the high-margin recurring subscription services (no content creation costs like Netflix (NFLX) and Spotify (SPOT)).

This creates a secondary growth derivative for Meta - a multi-billion-dollar subscription revenue stream layered on top of ads (est. 10% of total 2030 revenue) without cannibalizing its existing core ads business.

Thesis 3: Reality Labs Scaling — Ray-Ban Meta Glasses Volume Production in 2H26/2027 + AI-Agent Integration

I believe some investors might be discounting Meta’s prowess in AI smart glasses.

According to Counterpoint Research, Meta leads globally with a market share of 82% in 2H25, far ahead of competitors like Xiaomi and Apple, supported by strong demand for Ray-Ban Meta glasses (Gen 2) released in Sep last year. I believe market narratives are scornful of Meta being a Consumer AI giant.

But numbers don’t lie; Meta (EssilorLuxottica) sold 2.7 million AI glasses in 2024/25, respectively. Production capacity is expected to double to 20 million (or even 30 million) by year-end 2026, reflecting robust demand but only constrained by US supply shortages, which have delayed international launches to 2027.

Assuming a $360 ASP (based on Counterpoint) and 25 million AR smart glasses delivered in 2026, this will yield an $8-9 billion revenue run rate for Meta’s Reality Labs division (around 4-5% of 2025 revenue).

Finally, given Meta’s acquisition of Manus (December 2025) and Moltbook (March 2026), I believe Meta is effectively positioning itself to create an end-to-end AI agent ecosystem (ManusAI + Moltbook + Avocado).

I believe Meta’s acquisition of Manus/Moltbook enables Meta’s 3.5 billion MAU, or content creators, to leverage these tools and create personalized agents to research or execute multi-step tasks (content creation and posting on Instagram/Facebook) while opening the door to agent-to-agent and agent-to-human commerce/engagement loops.

I believe Meta will monetize its AI agents through premium subscriptions, API usage licensing for enterprises, and integration of AI agents into Ray-Ban Meta smart glasses

In a nutshell, when market narratives on Meta’s AI capabilities turn positive, I believe the stock might see an asymmetrical upside.

Valuation – Meta Looks Undervalued and Highly Discounted by the Market

Meta is trading at a forward FY27 P/E of 15x and a PEG of 0.78x, which is cheap relative to its 5-year P/E average of 22.6x, indicating undervaluation.

My DCF indicates that Meta is undervalued, with a target price of $760, implying a 42% upside (i.e., a 25-30% Margin of Safety).

This assumes:

  • Top-line growth of 24%/18%/17% from 2026/27/28, driven by AI driving strong ad prices and impression growth, alongside secondary growth derivatives from Instagram/Facebook/WhatsApp/AI agents subscription revenue and the successful ramp of Ray-Ban Meta glasses.
  • Operating Leverage, with OPM experiencing a small decline to 35% in 2026 due to higher R&D and employee expenses for AI talents, but gradually normalizing to around 39% by 2030.
  • CapEx scaling to $130-150 billion in 2026/27 for GPU purchases from NVDA & AMD/ASIC partnerships with Broadcom and data center buildouts (Louisiana and El Paso, Texas), before normalizing back to the $100-120 billion range by 2029/30.
  • A 9.1% WACC, based on its (1.29x Beta) and target EV/EBITDA multiple of 15x, in line with the long-term 5-year average.

I anticipate Meta to generate $4.4/13.8 billion of FCF in 2026/27 (FCF margin plummeting to 1.8%/4.7%) due to heavy data center capex investments before normalizing back to $72/116/119 billion of FCF in 2028/29/30 (FCF margins: 20.9%/29.3%/38.3%), in line with historical average, as Meta’s CapEx becomes more rational with strong high-margin and AI-driven ads and subscription business momentum after 2028.

Conclusion and Risks

On balance, I view Meta as an AI application beneficiary, underpinned by META’s

  1. Business efficiency and top-line acceleration potential from AI,
  2. Optionality from WhatsApp/Instagram/Facebook premium subscriptions,
  3. AI agents driving the E2E FOA ecosystem and AR glasses scaling.

Risks include 1) regulatory oversights from the EU and the US, 2) macroeconomic slowdown driving sluggish ad revenue, alongside 3) potential growth beyond guidance on AI Capital expenditure that will weigh on stock multiples.

That said, I remain a happy META shareholder, and I plan to add to the current weakness at $530.

data.title: Bigger Has Not Been Better For Glacier Bancorp
data.description:
This article serves as an update to my previous analysis for Glacier Bancorp, Inc. (GBCI), which was published on Seeking Alpha on October 11. At that time, I rated its stock as a Hold based on valuation concerns as well as a lack of a real catalyst to move its price forward. Since that first article was published, GBCI has a total return of -3.83%, just a couple of percentage points behind the S&P 500 but lagging the State Street SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF (KRE) by almost 10%.

Last June, Glacier Bancorp announced the acquisition of Guaranty Bancshares. The deal closed at the end of Q3 2025, marking GBCI’s 13th banking deal in the last decade. While the pace of deal-making makes it more challenging to fully appreciate the impact of each transaction individually, the Guaranty merger allowed Glacier to have greater access to attractive markets in the state of Texas. Unlike other regional banks that acquire smaller competitors and fold their operations under the name of the larger bank, Glacier uses a different model, allowing acquired companies to keep their names while their branches operate under one technology platform. Glacier Bancorp is now the holding company for 18 different banks, as shown in the slide below.

I do like the advantages that this model presents for smaller banks. Maintaining local brand names can give a branch a much more “community feel” and help prevent some bank customers from shopping around their accounts as they might if their bank changed brands. It also keeps customers from feeling as if they are being pushed into a larger banking system, even though Glacier now has a market cap of $5.7 billion.

GBCI is set to release its earnings for Q1 2026 on April 23. Based on the results from last year and the information the company shared in its investor presentation from February of this year, I still do not see a rush to create a new position in its stock. In this analysis, I will provide support for maintaining my Hold rating on Glacier Bancorp pending meaningful improvements to its operations and its bottom line in the year ahead.

Company Overview

Glacier Bancorp is the holding company for a collection of regional and community banks that have a geographic footprint that touches nine states from Texas to Washington. When looking at the loan balances from its branch locations, the largest shares of the company’s assets are found in Montana (28%), Idaho (14%), and Utah (13%). The Guaranty deal quickly moved Texas to fourth on that list at 10%. Further details about the company can be seen below, including its total assets, gross loans, and total deposits as of December 31, 2025.

Additional details about the company’s balance sheet data dating back over the last five years can be seen in the table below. Glacier provided this information as part of its 2025 10-K. While growth in several areas last year looks impressive, much of those increases can be attributed to the addition of Guaranty Bancshares. Over the last five years, the compound average growth rate for loans at the company is a healthy 9.3%, but total assets have only expanded 4.3% per year during this time as the company’s debt securities portfolio has decreased significantly.

Glacier Bancorp’s loan portfolio at the end of last year was heavily dependent on commercial real estate. CRE comprised 64.8% of the total loans receivable. The next closest loan segment was other commercial loans, which had an allocation of 16.7%. Residential real estate was third in weight at 11.7%, the only other loan segment above 5% for GBCI.

Glacier Bancorp pays a quarterly dividend of $0.33 per share and has paid out that same amount since its first regular distribution in 2022. As I noted in my first analysis, the payout ratio for GBCI is elevated, standing at 66.0% at the end of 2025. This decreases the likelihood that a dividend increase will be forthcoming for shareholders anytime soon. The stock’s forward yield of 3.02% is just six basis points behind the median for the financial sector.

Analysis Of Glacier Bancorp

Glacier Bancorp released its Q4 2025 and FY 2025 financial results back on January 22. This was the first full quarter that the company was able to show the results of its transaction for Guaranty Bancshares. Diluted earnings per share for Q4 were $0.49, a decrease of $0.08 per share, or 14%, from Q3. It was also a decline of $0.05, or 9%, from Q4 2024. Diluted earnings per share of $1.99 for FY 2025 were 18.5% better than FY 2024 but 1% worse than FY 2023.

The company’s performance on its bottom line has been disappointing for an extended period of time. It is noticeable that FY 2025 was the first year that Glacier has been able to show earnings growth since all the way back in FY 2021. The table below shows the performance of the bank during the last five years. The key to the turnaround for the company last year was its ability to grow interest income while decreasing interest expense.

If there is one area where the company has really made the most noticeable improvement in the last year, it would be net interest margin. After ending 2024 below 3%, NIM reached 3.58% in Q4 2025. The improvement of 61 basis points in NIM allowed Glacier to boost net interest income by $75 million, or 39%, when compared to Q4 of the previous year.

The remainder of the performance metrics for GBCI paint a mixed picture. Return on assets in Q4 2025 stood at 0.78%, a decline of 15 basis points from Q3 2025 and nine basis points from Q4 2024. Return on average equity was just 6.59% for FY 2025. This was an improvement from 2024’s final result of 6.02%, but it was also a far cry from 2022 and 2021, when the company’s ROE was above 10% in each year.

Asset quality for the combined performance from GBCI’s 18 banking brands appeared to be in decent shape at the end of 2025, even though the company recorded a sizeable increase in its provision for credit losses as the result of its merger activity. Net charge-offs of 0.06% in FY 2025 were two basis points lower than in FY 2024. The company’s ratio of non-performing assets did show a steady increase in FY 2025, as shown in the graph on the bottom right below, but the ratio is still lower than Glacier’s peers.

Even though GBCI has dropped about 15% since almost reaching $54 per share in early February, it still looks rather expensive. The stock trades at a trailing P/E of almost 22 and a forward P/E of over 14. The most recent tangible book value per share for the company is $21.01. At its current market price of $44.67 per share, GBCI currently trades at 2.13 times its TBV. The median price to TBV for a regional bank at the end of last year was about 1.4.

Risks To Consider

The Q1 2026 financial results from almost all regional banks are expected to show at least some of the impact of the recent uncertainty in the markets related to the conflict in the Middle East. Higher gas prices may have a meaningful impact on consumer spending and consumer savings. Higher long-term interest rates may impact businesses' ability to borrow and expand. The odds of the Federal Reserve cutting rates in the first half of this year have almost moved to zero. With no certain end in sight for most of these issues, their impacts may linger well into Q2 2026 as well. A downturn in the economy could increase nonperforming assets and cause a need to increase provisions for future losses. This is an issue for all regional banks and has been reflected in the declines seen in the State Street SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF since the end of the first week in February.

Glacier Bancorp actually closed two acquisitions in 2025, with the first being the Bank of Idaho back in May. While multiple deals closed in the same calendar year may eventually have meaningful long-term benefits for the company’s bottom line, there are integration risks that will be overhanging at least the first two quarters of this year. Complications with system conversions, employee turnover, and a chance that expected cost savings will not be achieved are all possible challenges for the company in 2026.

There is also a chance that the company will initiate yet another deal this year, complicating matters further. As part of its Investor Presentation from February, Glacier was not shy about identifying potential targets in both the Mountain West and Southwest regions, as shown in the slide below. The company pointed out that population growth in the Mountain West is expected to be about 3.6% higher than the national average over the next five years. In the Southwest, population growth is projected to be 5.3% greater than the US average of just 0.3%.

All of this M&A activity is having an impact on the company’s balance sheet, as goodwill increased 31.1% in the last 12 months to $1.38 billion as of December 31, 2025. The total amount of goodwill on the books for GBCI is now 32.7% of its total stockholders’ equity. If goodwill that is recorded with Glacier’s previous or future transactions becomes impaired, the company could face accounting charges that impact both its earnings and capital position.

The Bottom Line

As it did back in October, Seeking Alpha's quant rating deems GBCI to be a Hold, although its overall score put it in the Sell category at the start of this year. The stock does receive an above-average score in growth, partially due to the revenue expansion that is expected for FY 2026. The below-average performance in ROA and ROE is a big reason why the stock receives a “C-” in profitability.

Without a compelling reason to believe the stock will outperform its regional bank peers this year, I am maintaining the Hold recommendation that I stated last fall. If the company's asset quality metrics were lower, I could even see justification for a sell rating here. The valuation for GBCI is just not compelling given its rather average performance over the last few years. It will be interesting to see if the company can resist the temptation in the first half of 2026 to become active in the M&A space once again so that investors may have greater visibility on the full impact of its most recent transactions.

data.title: Netflix: Focus On Content, Focus On Its Own
data.description:
At the start of March, I concluded that Netflix (NFLX) was moving ahead on its own after it declined to raise its offer for Warner Bros., thereby effectively pulling out of the bidding war for the business.

I liked the financial discipline displayed, and despite some distractions, Netflix was able to obtain a nice multi-billion termination fee, while it left its peer Paramount Skydance (PSKY) with much more leverage. This, given the recent events (including bond markets), might actually provide an opportunity for Netflix.

That is clearly seen in the share price of Paramount, which has fallen from levels around $13 early in March to lows of $9 today. The weakening of such a competitor, facing a huge debt load in uncertain macro times, is of course a positive for Netflix here. This gives the company more relative power to invest in content or perhaps acquire assets down the road at less competitive prices.

Netflix continues on its own, and following the company quickly returning to focus on internal content, I like the strategic route walked here.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent M&A efforts, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

Going After It Alone

After missing out on Warner Bros., after initially offering $82 billion to acquire the firm, Netflix pulled out of the bidding war for Warner Bros. about a month ago. Co-CEOs Sarandos and Peters stressed financial discipline, quickly pointing out $20 billion in content spending on series and films in 2026. This is designed to create superior competitive offerings and continue to take market share organically, instead of acquiring an iconic studio with all the pricing, integration, and leverage risks associated.

Concerns about such dealmaking and the prospects of a bidding war made that a $130 stock fell to the $100 mark in December and the $80s earlier this year. Shares have now recovered to levels around $95 per share, which is after news about Netflix pulling back broke. Investors were fearful about large M&A, assumption of debt, and different corporate cultures to be integrated, let alone regulatory risk out here.

Meanwhile, Netflix reported a solid 18% increase in fourth-quarter sales to $12.1 billion, with the company ending the year with 375 million subscribers. The company guided for growth to slow down to 15% in the first quarter of 2026, still a solid achievement. Full-year sales were reported at $45 billion, including a $1.5 billion advertising business, while 2026 sales were seen up 12-14% to a midpoint of $51.2 billion.

With operating margins seen around 30.5%, plus or minus a percent, all this created a solid roadmap for operating earnings to come in at $16.1 billion. After applying taxes, earnings could be seen around $3 per share, translating into a 32 times earnings multiple for a largely unleveraged business. Just over $5 billion in net debt is very manageable, set to come down following receipt of a $2.8 billion termination fee.

I like that the business was going after its future alone, as it has done very successfully in the past. However, outside all the opportunities and challenges ahead, there were other risks, such as the rise of AI. Of course, this could help in reducing the price of content; it could be a real concern as well. Given all that, Netflix has gotten a bit more compelling, but that is not the same as seeing great appeal just yet.

Upping The Content Game

To bolster the content offerings, Netflix was not sitting still and quickly moved to make some content announcements. By the end of March, Netflix was reportedly looking to double coverage of National Football League games, this, however, just being a 4-package game.

The key in this is, of course, the rise of sports and live content, both of which are fairly rare in the case of the content library of Netflix. A couple of such deals quickly result in Netflix hitting the $20 billion mark, after it spent $17.1 billion in addition to content assets in 2025. In all fairness, the company is depreciating such content at nearly the same pace here, as discussions about capex and amortization schemes were quite lively in the past.

To pay for such content, Netflix is reportedly looking to raise prices as well, with most of such recent price hikes dating back to the start of 2025. This is likely going to be a key driver behind the anticipated revenue growth this year.

Somewhat ironic and perhaps painful is that Netflix actually teamed up with Warner Music to collaborate on movies and documentaries of renowned musicians and songwriters. Of course, Warner Music no longer has ties to Warner Bros., unlike in the past.

And Now?

Given all these trends, Netflix has quickly found itself in calmer waters. Following the decisive move to pull out of the bidding war, the focus has quickly and decisively shifted to the company's own operations. This results in new content deals, more diversified content deals, which look like the way to go to drive quality and diversified appeal.

This makes me upbeat from an operational and strategic perspective, as a low-thirty times earnings multiple marks a premium. It comes amidst limited leverage, strong growth, and undisputed leadership in the category for attention and entertainment, as the value of Netflix given the time spent is relatively modest. Furthermore, compared to alternatives like television and cable, the pricing of subscriptions still sounds very reasonable in my view.

This could drive continued growth this year, as many have real concerns about the uncertainty of AI, which, of course, could be helpful, but I fear the potential negative impact here. In the bull case, it could help reduce the cost of content creation, but the negatives are potentially much more severe. These have the potential to unleash a wide range of cheaper competitors, at least in terms of content creation, which of course is not the same as distribution.

It is just that concern that prevents me from pulling the trigger, yet any continuation of growth and pullback to the lower $80s could be used as a buying opportunity here.

data.title: Nike Q3: Turnaround Plan Turns Investors Away, Shares Attractive
data.description:
Shares in sneaker and apparel company Nike (NKE) have been taking a drumming of late. The release of its Q3 results only added further pressure to the underperformance.

At current levels, I view this as an attractive opportunity for long-term positioning in a company whose turnaround hasn’t necessarily materialized just yet but is bound to happen in time.

In reporting its Q3, NKE said that it's expecting sales to decline through the end of 2026, with a 2% to 4% decline in its fiscal Q4. That’s a significant miss for about 2% growth.

With U.S. markets already on edge over the ongoing conflict in the Middle East and the associated spike in oil prices, it’s perhaps not a surprise that weary investors were taking no chances with what some could perhaps call a ‘falling knife’ stock.

I, on the other hand, see the double-digit declines as an attractive opportunity to initiate new positioning or to add to an existing one.

NKE Stock Key Metrics

Shares in NKE were already down about 20% over the past year prior to the release of its Q3. Add on another approximately 10% decline post-earnings, and you have NKE trading at historically low pricing.

While the Seeking Alpha (“SA”) quants have never really graded NKE favorably on valuation, I have begged to differ, seeing its price-to-sales ratio as a key indicator of its valuation.

While NKE has certainly struggled with its brand in relation to rising brands, such as HOKA, which is owned by Deckers Outdoor (DECK), NKE is still a global market leader in footwear, and I don’t see that changing anytime soon.

For a market leader such as NKE, I see a sub-1.5x price/sales ratio as overly pessimistic, especially when a competitor such as DECK is trading at nearly double that valuation.

Analysts across SA are also similarly neutral or pessimistic on the stock.

The most recent coverage just before the Q3 release was actually quite timely, and the call is commendable. However, NKE has bounced back before after trading at lows, and I believe it can do so again.

NKE Q3 Results Recap

In my view, it would be a mistake to focus too much on NKE’s headline numbers for Q3. I believe what is more important is what the management team is deliberately doing beneath the surface.

At the topline, revenues were flat as reported and down 3% on a currency-neutral basis. EPS, meanwhile, came in at $0.35/share. Both were above expectations. That’s a bit of good news to digest.

Gross margin, on the other hand, fell 40.2%, pressured heavily by tariffs and elevated markdown activity across channels.

At first glance, this looks uninspiring. But listening in to the earnings call, it was clear to me that much of this was likely intentional.

During the earnings call, for example, CEO Elliott Hill repeatedly emphasized the company’s “Win Now” actions, which include aggressively clearing aged and unhealthy inventory from classic footwear franchises. That decision alone created a roughly 5-point headwind to results.

In my view, NKE is willingly sacrificing near-term revenue to reset the marketplace, clean up discounting, and restore brand health.

There were also bright spots. NKE Running grew over 20% globally, wholesale in North America jumped 11%, and performance categories like football, training, and basketball showed signs of life.

This was offset, however, by Sportswear, China, and Converse, all of which remain early in the turnaround and continue to weigh on results.

The company also took a +$230M severance charge to right-size supply chain and technology costs. The charge is significant, but I believe it positions margins for recovery beginning in FY27.

Overall, this wasn’t a good quarter. It was a necessary one.

What Is The Outlook For NKE Stock?

If the market was looking for any reassurance, NKE’s guidance did the opposite.

From a transparency standpoint, NKE did make it very clear that the pressure we saw in Q3 is not ending anytime soon.

Looking ahead, the company expects revenue to be down low single digits through the end of the calendar year, with fiscal Q4 sales projected to fall 2% to 4%. That’s well below prior expectations for modest growth, and this is likely a key reason shares sold off so sharply.

The biggest drag continues to be Greater China, where Q4 revenue is expected to plunge roughly 20% as NKE intentionally reduces sell-in, cleans up the digital channel, and removes aged inventory from the marketplace.

These actions are expected to remain a headwind through fiscal 2027. At the same time, elevated tariffs will continue to pressure gross margins into the first quarter of fiscal 2027 before improvement begins in Q2.

From a more positive standpoint, North America is expected to post modest growth in Q4, wholesale momentum is building, and management expects gross margin expansion to begin later this year as promotional intensity eases and inventory health improves.

I believe the forward outlook is messy in the near term by design.

In my view, NKE is choosing to endure short-term pain now in order to reset the foundation for more profitable, sustainable growth ahead. Whether investors can actually buy into that story is another matter.

Is NKE Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

For me, this is where the opportunity becomes clear.

In my view, NKE is not missing because demand for the brand has permanently eroded. It is missing because the current management team is actively dismantling parts of the business that were propping up revenue in an unhealthy way. That’s a big distinction to me.

The company is choosing to take the pain now rather than let discounting, bloated inventory, and an overextended digital push continue to dilute the brand.

Investors, understandably, don’t like hearing that sales will decline through 2026 and that China will remain a drag well into fiscal 2027. Yes, that creates uncertainty, and uncertainty gets sold. But this is exactly the type of fear that has historically created attractive long-term entry points in market leaders.

At a sub-1.5x price-to-sales ratio, with wholesale momentum returning, performance categories growing, and margin recovery expected to begin next year, I see a company rebuilding its foundation while the market focuses only on the near-term mess.

Turnarounds rarely look pretty while they’re happening. In my view, NKE is in the uncomfortable spectrum of one. That said, for investors willing to look past the next few quarters, I view the recent pullback as a compelling buying opportunity rather than a reason to step aside.
